name: Ph.D. in Chemical Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight referenced courses expose the classic chemical-engineering doctoral core (CHE 611 Thermodynamics, CHE 612 Kinetics and Reactor Design, CHE 624 Transport Phenomena I, and CHE 626 Mathematical Methods) plus the standard research spine of CHE 791 Graduate Seminar, CHE 792 Pre-Doctoral Research, and CHE 705 Independent Study. The core is well chosen and internally consistent, but no plan of study was parsed (requirementGroups 0) and no advanced electives, qualifying-exam sequence, or Transport II appear, so progression beyond the first-year core is not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a term-by-term doctoral plan of study that shows the required core, the qualifying-exam milestone, an advanced elective menu, and the dissertation-research sequence so the path from coursework to candidacy is visible.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is flat (deepestPrereqChain 0) because every core course states its prerequisite in prose against undergraduate preparation (e.g., CHE 611 requires 'undergraduate courses in physical chemistry and thermodynamics'), which is reasonable for a Ph.D. entry cohort but yields no machine-readable chain. One dangling reference remains (missingCourseCount 1, CHE 790A), almost certainly a doctoral dissertation-research course absent from the parsed catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the dangling CHE 790A reference (add or correct the dissertation-research course code) and, where possible, cite specific gateway course codes rather than prose so the doctoral prerequisite structure is explicit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or doctoral educational objectives appear anywhere in the data, which is a genuine gap for a Ph.D. page where research competencies, breadth requirements, and candidacy expectations should be stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit Ph.D. learning outcomes (research design, disciplinary breadth across thermodynamics/kinetics/transport, scholarly communication) and map them to the core courses and dissertation milestones.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta cannot be computed (no plan groups parsed), so a prospective doctoral student cannot see the total post-B.S. credit load or the split between coursework, seminar, and dissertation research. CHE 791 is correctly shown as a 0-credit seminar, but the overall credit picture is opaque for a degree that typically runs on the order of 75 post-baccalaureate credits.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and its distribution across required core, electives, seminar, and pre-doctoral/dissertation research so the coursework-to-research balance is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific for the four academic core courses (e.g., CHE 624's 'unified treatment of molecular and turbulent momentum, energy, and mass transport'), and titles clearly signal function. However the page carries no plan of study, no stated total credits, and a dangling CHE 790A, so a reader must infer structure from a short course list.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a structured plan-of-study section and a credit summary, and clean the dangling course reference, to complement the strong individual course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects timeless, essential chemical-engineering fundamentals, and CHE 612 notably foregrounds 'small-scale pharmaceutical production,' aligning with New Jersey's pharma corridor. The visible curriculum is nonetheless light on modern computational and data-driven methods, molecular/multiscale simulation, and process-systems or sustainability content that now define frontier chemical-engineering research.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or surface a required or elective modern-methods course (e.g., molecular simulation or machine learning for chemical systems) so the doctoral core signals contemporary computational practice alongside the classical fundamentals.

descriptionQuality.justification: The academic core descriptions are substantive and specific rather than placeholder (CHE 626 details formulating models and deriving analytical and numerical solutions to steady/unsteady problems; CHE 611 covers thermodynamic functions applied to processes). The research/administrative entries (CHE 705, 791, 792) read more as registration rules than content, slightly lowering the aggregate.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated but mostly as prose against undergraduate preparation (deepestPrereqChain 0), which is defensible for doctoral admission yet not unambiguous course-code gating; one dangling reference persists (missingCourseCount 1, CHE 790A), and several research courses gate on advisor permission rather than coursework.

missingTopics.rationale: The core stops at CHE 624 Transport Phenomena I and CHE 626 Mathematical Methods with no molecular dynamics, DFT, or multiscale-modeling course, now standard doctoral tooling for chemical-engineering research.

missingTopics.topic: Data science and machine learning for chemical systems
missingTopics.rationale: No course in statistical learning or ML for process/materials data appears, despite the department already offering MTEN 631/633 that a Ph.D. plan could cross-list.
Kinetics and reactor design (CHE 612) are present, but there is no visible optimization, control, or flowsheet-modeling course to connect unit fundamentals to whole-process design.
Only Transport I (CHE 624) is exposed; a doctoral core normally continues to a second transport course covering interphase and turbulent transport in depth.
No electrochemistry, catalysis-for-energy, or decarbonization course appears, topics central to current chemical-engineering research funding and hiring.

emergingTrends.rationale: Physics-informed and data-driven surrogates are reshaping reactor design and transport modeling; CHE 626 Mathematical Methods is the natural home to introduce them.
Chemical-engineering research is pivoting to carbon capture and electrified processes, which the thermodynamics/kinetics core (CHE 611/612) could anchor with an applied elective.
Given CHE 612's explicit pharmaceutical framing and the department's PHEN courses, continuous manufacturing and PAT are a natural doctoral research direction to formalize.
Bayesian-optimization-driven autonomous labs are accelerating catalysis and materials discovery and would strengthen the kinetics/reactor research area.
Chemical-engineering doctoral programs increasingly span cell-based and enzymatic processes; a bioreaction elective would broaden the reactor-design core toward this growth area.

topSuggestions: Publish a doctoral plan of study with total credits, the qualifying-exam milestone, an advanced-elective menu, and the dissertation-research sequence to replace the null statedTotalCredits and empty requirementGroups.
State explicit Ph.D. learning outcomes (research design, disciplinary breadth, scholarly communication) and map them to the core and dissertation milestones.
Resolve the dangling CHE 790A reference and cite specific gateway course codes where possible to make the prerequisite structure explicit.
Add a required or elective modern-methods course (molecular simulation and/or machine learning for chemical systems) to modernize the classical core.
Extend the exposed core to Transport Phenomena II and a process-systems/optimization course so progression beyond the first year is visible.
